fields.Identifying_Data_Chief_Complaint: 14-year-old male brought by school counselor after expressing thoughts that his family 'would be better off if I wasn't around' following father's deployment notification.
fields.Subjective: Patient is a 14-year-old male from a military family presenting after telling his school counselor he has been thinking his family would be better off without him since learning his father received deployment orders to an active combat zone two weeks ago. Patient describes passive thoughts of not wanting to be alive, wishing he could 'fade away,' but has no plan, method, or intent to harm himself. He reports feeling like a burden to his mother who is also active duty military and will be managing the household alone during father's 12-month deployment. This will be father's third deployment, but patient reports this one feels different because the destination is more dangerous and he is old enough now to understand the risks. He has had difficulty sleeping, often lying awake worrying about his father being killed. Appetite is normal. He continues playing on his school soccer team but reports less enjoyment. He has close friends, many also from military families, and states they understand but he does not want to burden them with his worries. No prior mental health treatment or concerning thoughts. (Social Worker Lieutenant Marcus Brown, LCSW)

Mental_Status_Examination.Appearance: Neat, age-appropriate, military family sticker on backpack
Mental_Status_Examination.Thought_Content: Passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent; anxiety about deployment

fields.Diagnostic_Impression: Adjustment disorder with mixed anxiety and depressed mood related to parental military deployment; passive suicidal ideation.
fields.Psychosocial_Context: Military family; father deploying to combat zone for 12 months; mother also active duty; previous deployment experiences; peer support from other military families; active in soccer.
Assessment_and_Plan: Low risk: passive SI without plan or intent in context of anticipated separation from father deploying to danger.
Strong protective factors: both parents' support, military peer community, soccer engagement, good insight, resilient coping history.
Normal stress response to military family deployment challenges.
Safety plan created with patient and counselor.
Referral to military family services and counseling for deployment support.
Connect with on-base youth programs specializing in deployment transitions.
Encourage continued peer support; normalize deployment-related anxiety.
Mother educated on monitoring and provided resources for military families.
Regular communication plan with father during deployment when operationally feasible.
Follow-up with military family counselor within one week.
Safe for discharge with mother and military support systems.
fields.Author: Lieutenant Marcus Brown, LCSW (Social Worker)
